a function over a collection"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2023-05-24T08:26:28Z","updated_at":"2024-11-04T09:38:59Z","closed_at":"2024-11-04T09:38:59Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I am a newcomer to Julia language. However, I found something strange about @fastmath and @. .

This problem happens when slices of matrices or vectors are involved. Please consider the following case:
```
julia> a = rand(Float64, 2, 10)
2×10 Matrix{Float64}:
 0.670906  0.160401  0.626289  0.391141  0.359202  0.546171  0.382683  0.276164     0.0326305  0.288932
 0.102545  0.316434  0.306069  0.919442  0.943035  0.516146  0.710773  0.000468101  0.428014   0.305994

julia> @fastmath @. a[1, 1:2:10] += 1.0
ERROR: UndefVarError: ##312 not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ fastmath.jl:116
```
However, the following code works:
```
 @fastmath a[1, 1:2:10] .+= 1.0
5-element view(::Matrix{Float64}, 1, 1:2:9) with eltype Float64:
 1.6709058653769435
 1.6262886999132795
 1.3592019776966817
 1.3826830585942695
 1.0326304999494327

julia> a
2×10 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.67091   0.160401  1.62629   0.391141  1.3592    0.546171  1.38268   0.276164     1.03263   0.288932
 0.102545  0.316434  0.306069  0.919442  0.943035  0.516146  0.710773  0.000468101  0.428014  0.305994
```
When no slices are involved, this problem will disapper:
```
@fastmath @. a += 1.0
2×10 Matrix{Float64}:
 2.67091  1.1604   2.62629  1.39114  2.3592   1.54617  2.38268  1.27616  2.03263  1.28893
 1.10255  1.31643  1.30607  1.91944  1.94303  1.51615  1.71077  1.00047  1.42801  1.30599
```

My platform is Julia 1.8.1 on Window 11 version 22H2 (OS Build 22621.1.1702). The same problem appears for Julia 1.8.5 on WSL 2 Ubuntu 20.04 LTS.

Is this a feature of the language? or is it a bug?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49945/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49945/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

am a newcomer to Julia language. However, I found something strange about @fastmath and @. .

This problem happens when slices of matrices or vectors are involved. Please consider the following case:
```
julia> a = rand(Float64, 2, 10)
2×10 Matrix{Float64}:
 0.670906  0.160401  0.626289  0.391141  0.359202  0.546171  0.382683  0.276164     0.0326305  0.288932
 0.102545  0.316434  0.306069  0.919442  0.943035  0.516146  0.710773  0.000468101  0.428014   0.305994

julia> @fastmath @. a[1, 1:2:10] += 1.0
ERROR: UndefVarError: ##312 not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ fastmath.jl:116
```
However, the following code works:
```
 @fastmath a[1, 1:2:10] .+= 1.0
5-element view(::Matrix{Float64}, 1, 1:2:9) with eltype Float64:
 1.6709058653769435
 1.6262886999132795
 1.3592019776966817
 1.3826830585942695
 1.0326304999494327

julia> a
2×10 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.67091   0.160401  1.62629   0.391141  1.3592    0.546171  1.38268   0.276164     1.03263   0.288932
 0.102545  0.316434  0.306069  0.919442  0.943035  0.516146  0.710773  0.000468101  0.428014  0.305994
```
When no slices are involved, this problem will disapper:
```
@fastmath @. a += 1.0
2×10 Matrix{Float64}:
 2.67091  1.1604   2.62629  1.39114  2.3592   1.54617  2.38268  1.27616  2.03263  1.28893
 1.10255  1.31643  1.30607  1.91944  1.94303  1.51615  1.71077  1.00047  1.42801  1.30599
```

My platform is Julia 1.8.1 on Window 11 version 22H2 (OS Build 22621.1.1702). The same problem appears for Julia 1.8.5 on WSL 2 Ubuntu 20.04 LTS.

Is this a feature of the language? or is it a However, I found something strange about @fastmath and @. .

This problem happens when slices of matrices or vectors are involved. Please consider the following case:
```
julia> a = rand(Float64, 2, 10)
2×10 Matrix{Float64}:
 0.670906  0.160401  0.626289  0.391141  0.359202  0.546171  0.382683  0.276164     0.0326305  0.288932
 0.102545  0.316434  0.306069  0.919442  0.943035  0.516146  0.710773  0.000468101  0.428014   0.305994

julia> @fastmath @. a[1, 1:2:10] += 1.0
ERROR: UndefVarError: ##312 not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ fastmath.jl:116
```
However, the following code works:
```
 @fastmath a[1, 1:2:10] .+= 1.0
5-element view(::Matrix{Float64}, 1, 1:2:9) with eltype Float64:
 1.6709058653769435
 1.6262886999132795
 1.3592019776966817
 1.3826830585942695
 1.0326304999494327

julia> a
2×10 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.67091   0.160401  1.62629   0.391141  1.3592    0.546171  1.38268   0.276164     1.03263   0.288932
 0.102545  0.316434  0.306069  0.919442  0.943035  0.516146  0.710773  0.000468101  0.428014  0.305994
```
When no slices are involved, this problem will disapper:
```
@fastmath @. a += 1.0
2×10 Matrix{Float64}:

```

My platform is Julia 1.8.1 on Window 11 version 22H2 (OS Build 22621.1.1702). The same problem appears for Julia 1.8.5 on WSL 2 Ubuntu 20.04 LTS.

Thank you for the reply. Then I will make sure macros are not used together in my codes.

As for why '@fastmath' appears in front of '@.', the reason is that I put '@fastmath' before the definition of the function, that is,

'@fastmath function myfunc()', and the '@.' macro is used inside the function.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1560964364/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```
 0.102545  0.316434  0.306069  0.919442  0.943035  0.516146  0.710773  0.000468101  0.428014   0.305994

julia> @fastmath @. a[1, 1:2:10] += 1.0
ERROR: UndefVarError: ##312 not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ fastmath.jl:116
```
However, the following code works:
```
 @fastmath a[1, 1:2:10] .+= 1.0
5-element view(::Matrix{Float64}, 1, 1:2:9) with eltype Float64:
 1.6709058653769435
 1.6262886999132795
 1.3592019776966817
 1.3826830585942695
 1.0326304999494327

julia> a
2×10 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.67091   0.160401  1.62629   0.391141  1.3592    0.546171  1.38268   0.276164     1.03263   0.288932
 0.102545  0.316434  0.306069  0.919442  0.943035  0.516146  0.710773  0.000468101  0.428014  0.305994
```
When no slices are involved, this problem will disapper:
```
@fastmath @. a += 1.0
2×10 Matrix{Float64}:
 2.67091  1.1604   2.62629  1.39114  2.3592   1.54617  2.38268  1.27616  2.03263  1.28893
 1.10255  1.31643  1.30607  1.91944  1.94303  1.51615  1.71077  1.00047  1.42801  1.30599
```

My platform is Julia 1.8.1 on Window 11 version 22H2 (OS Build 22621.1.1702). The same problem appears for Julia 1.8.5 on WSL 2 Ubuntu 20.04 LTS.

Is this a feature of the language? or is it a bug?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49945/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49945/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1560967440","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49945#issuecomment-1560967440","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49945","id":1560967440,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5dCnkQ","user":{"login":"Tree-Yang","id":16078543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2MDc4NTQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/16078543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tree-Yang","html_url":"https://github.com/Tree-Yang","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tree-Yang/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tree-Yang/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tree-Yang/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tree-Yang/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tree-Yang/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tree-Yang/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tree-Yang/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tree-Yang/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tree-Yang/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-05-24T11:44:52Z","updated_at":"2023-05-24T11:44:52Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> in general you can't stick two macros in front of each other and expect them to magically work.

Thank you for the reply. Then I will make sure macros are not used together in my codes.

As for why '@fastmath' appears in front of '@.', the reason is that I put '@fastmath' before the definition of the function, that is,

'@fastmath function myfunc()', and the '@.' macro is used inside the function.






> > Is this a feature of the language? or is it a bug?
> 
> Can't say it's a feature, but as a matter of fact macros aren't composable, in general you can't stick two macros in front of each other and expect them to magically work, because macros can do arbitrary syntax manipulation and one macro may be changing under the feet of the other macro the expression that it sees.
> 
> Side note, `@fastmath` doesn't do anything with broadcasting anyway, see
> 
> ```
> julia> @macroexpand @fastmath a[1, 1:2:10] .+= 1.0
> :(a[1, 1:2:10] .+= 1.0)
> ```
